I’m not sure I want to literally block out all of my time each week with a calendar. Something feels robotic and unhuman about it. I think we shouldn’t just fly by the seat of our pants but this advise seems like a way to cope with the system instead of pulling back from it. Most people will notice when they begin to set boundaries and resist even just a little they will be threatened with their livelihood being taken away, or they’ll be warned that they aren’t being a “team player.” The reason they can do this is because they have us by our throats. The job we have is our lifeline and getting another one often isn’t easy or quick. It can bankrupt you to resist even a little. The owners know this and use it to keep you obedient and productive. What’s weird is when you stop and ask yourself, “what the hell am I even producing?”

Thank you! I feel so guilty about being burnt out even though I'm just a stay-at-home parent. I told this a therapist once and he stopped me right there, pointing out that I don't get to leave my job site and that's hard in its own, valid way. My husband is burnt out as well, especially since he's been doing a lot of overtime, so I try to do all the cooking, housework, and most of the parenting. The issue is that as a neurodivergent person I've struggled with normal life even before I became a parent, but potentially having to give up on my lifelong big family dream hurts too. But yeah, it seems like more than either of us can handle. I try to take things one step at a time for now because I don't want to deal with more, but I'm no good at that either. I don't know where I'm going with this anymore and I think I'm just pushing off getting chores done and going to bed, so I'll stop. I'm too tired to focus anyways. Edit: I love my toddler and unborn little acrobat-baby more than I've ever loved anyone on this Earth though. It's weird to love and yet struggle so much with this whole parenting thing.

Somehow I was very confused when watching this. Questions I had immediately was "wait, what's the deadline of these tasks? What if you don't know what the topic is going to be before they told you to write a lecture in 6 hours? What if you didn't have a choice to do a certain stupid task any other day than the day the boss told you to do it? How is it you have room to plan your work like this?" Then I realized; this video doesn't apply to people with a regular job. Regular jobs have strict deadlines, and usually piss poor management. You couldn't do all the stuff Dr K. is talking about in a regular job. It seems that while Dr K. still works for people to a degree, he has a lot more control over his topics and deadlines, room to stretch, if you will. Not so in most other places. I think this would apply better for people doing independent work, or at least jobs that have a high level of autonomy, both of which are *very* privileged positions. I don't see this video helping most people.

For the longest time I thought burnout just meant “being tired.” But when it hit me, I realized it’s way deeper, it’s when your brain feels foggy, your body is drained, and even things you used to love feel empty. I kept blaming myself, thinking I just needed more discipline, but the truth is I was stuck in survival mode. What helped me wasn’t pushing harder, but giving myself permission to reset. I tried a 30-day plan that focused on energy, sleep, and focus in realistic steps, and slowly I started feeling like myself again. Mornings didn’t feel so heavy, and I could finally enjoy small things without guilt. If burnout feels like your “normal,” don’t ignore it. The guide that helped me was Reset Your Body and Mind by Morgan Lane, and it honestly gave me the structure I needed to recover instead of sinking deeper.

As an healthcare/SEND coordinator, I get asked about burnout a lot. If you're passionate and into your job/career then burnout isnt as likely as if you're doing something just to get by. I LOVE the satisfaction of getting a child with severe needs the right person to work with them, knowing that I'm only called on once all other options are exhausted and there is nothing more fulfilling than working on a child for a month and then speaking to the parents about how well they're getting on now. 7am - 6pm Mon - Fri, 12 - 5pm most Saturdays (I really do enjoy my work. My partner doesn't even mind because I'm tired but not burnt after I finish, and I do still find time for the gym. Work a job to make ends meet sure, but work on yourself to find yourself in a job that you find fulfilment in. If that means working in the local to make ends meet while you study then do it. The sooner you get that out the way, the sooner you can start living the life you want

I feel burnt out in a different way. I got laid off 2.5 months ago and it's been nonstop effort to stay on task with applying to places that I know I'm not going to hear back from, working on my self in the meantime through healing modalities like journaling, reading books (by authors like Wiest), listenig to podcasts and youtube videos like Heidi's, trying to become attuned to my body and what it needs...and most days I just want to sleep the day away. My partner, the love of my life, who is and has been everything to me for my healing and growth, and I went on a break almost a month ago because of how shaky the ground is right now, and I'm not sure where that's going to end up, and it's devastating me. I know I just have to stay present with myself, but I feel so dysregulated all day and I'm just so freaking burnt out of all of it... except for friends. Going to lunch and doing activities with them has been a lifeline I never knew I needed so badly. I've found almost a calling or purpose from pouring into others. I have been leaning on them for support for the past several weeks and it's been so healing for me to have such wonderful people in my life, helping me get through this really tough time. I'm still just...so exhausted from it all.

Definitely agree that burnout leads to becoming fragile and less resilient to times of being pushed too hard, whether by yourself, others, or whatever life throws at you. I'm still overcoming burnout, and personally for me the crashes come from taking things a bit too easy to try to recover or by me making the same mistakes that seemed so harmless before like putting in that extra hour or two of work, or putting in more effort throughout the day so that I can try to get ahead. It sounds counter intuitive, but my solution is to put the brakes on being an over achiever. Like with anyone, being an overachiever led to a lot of success for me earlier. I got addicted to it but did it to much to the point where I literally have to re-train my brain to what a "normal" amount of daily work is again to fix what I got going on. Some of the weird struggles I face right now is trying to find that sweet spot of not taking things too easy with work to where it puts me in a low energy and lethargic state of being, but not working too much either. Like sometimes I have a hard time figuring out whether the times I crash are from either me taking things way too lathargically easy or from the times I unknowingly pushed myself too hard. Being consistent with the recovery isnt always easy, but it is getting easier over time and I have no doubt I'll get over it eventually. It's just pretty annoying in the meantime. Thought I would just share my experience in case others might find it useful because it took me forever to pin down exactly what my problems and solutions are just because of how paradoxical the nature of it all is.

This video clearly explained how burnout isn’t just being tired - it’s a mix of emotional, mental, and even physical exhaustion. I could feel that in my own life. I’ve been pushing myself hard - staying up late, taking on extra things because I felt like I had to prove I was good enough. But the video pointed out that what we need isn’t more hustle - it’s rest, balance, and clear boundaries. It made me realize pushing through burnout by working harder is exactly what creates more burnout. Now I’m trying to set limits: I’m saying no more often, scheduling real breaks, and trying to sleep earlier. It’s been a shift, but I already feel lighter. The takeaway is that you don’t have to prove anything to anyone - sometimes the strongest thing you can do is step back and rest.

I kept telling myself I just needed to rest more. Take a weekend off, sleep in, maybe meditate. But I still felt heavy like my brain was stuck in survival mode. I feel like this is why our emotions fluctuate, especially in your relationships with people, especially when you feel you can’t be this certain part of yourself, but that’s because you’re not communicating, you’re not talking, and you won’t know what’s best to communicate or talk about without experience, you do need to trial and error these things, and you won’t know if you don’t try, no relationship or choice is perfect it never will be, but it is what you make of it, you need to water that garden and mess around with that works with that specific soil it won’t be the same all the time, you will get it wrong, you lack the knowledge and you will only grow if you try. Something I’ve been thinking about is, how do married couples stay together for so long, how, they must get burned out and lose feelings for each other at some point right, and I think it’s about balance and understanding that yes nothing will last forever, emotions fluctuate for multiple reasons yes life happens but it’s about choice, it’s about choosing that this right here is worth pouring into, like a job that you really want and are passionate about and you go right into it, there’s a reason why you wanted it maybe it was like a crush , a lack of information, but then you get it and you grow to love it and see it for what it is and you’ve invested all of this time into it, which must mean it matters right (like when you’re growing your garden, nothing seems to work right, so you give up , and move on to the next garden only to realise if you had just kept looking it genuinely could have worked, but it’s okay don’t make the same mistake with the next garden, because maybe the soil was just rotten in the previous garden and you didn’t have the resources or knowledge to be the one to nurture it), we as humans do change we do , that is a fact but we need to grow with our surroundings, we don’t outgrow things, at least that’s what I believe, I think we grow to resent them or we grow tired because thats all you will be doing and you have neglected all these other parts of yourself, why leave a secure job because you’re tired of it or your feel you cannot be yourself in it, when you can take a step back maybe not pour as much energy into it and balance it out, pour into it still but not with the same energy find the balance, it doesn’t have to be one or the other, we grow yes, we don’t become completely different people tho, the things we liked before still matter, and I think it’s important exploring why you liked them to begin with, maybe it was for the security, the stability, the thrill it gives you, how fulfilling it was, and because you’re tired or burnt out you no longer want it you want the thrill of the next thing (but again you’ll find yourself running into the same issues because you’re not actually getting to the root, that’s why it’s so important to be online with your deeper most inner thoughts, not just listening to the subconscious, it’s important to understand why work the way you do, so you can show up in the rest of the world in a responsible way), we just gain information and understand that certain things cannot be the way they used to be because they’re unhealthy and or are setting us back, but i genuinely think it’s mindset, and I’m no expert I’m still learning these things myself. I genuinely just think we give up on things too easily nowadays, yes live life and always do your best to be happy, but you’ll notice you keep disregarding things and growing out of them because you are pouring way too much way too early and not thinking about the steps and the journey, it won’t be easy, you have to see things for what they are and live in the moment and sometimes it’s good to have those cute little fantasies, just not one or the other you know, things will not fall on your lap you need to put in the effort, you won’t just wake up healed, you have to study and do research and hit walls and redirect and even then you’ll realise you’ve been doing it wrong and might have to restart, I genuinely believe the most fulfilling way to live life is through gratitude, you have the privilege of being here, although not perfectly, we all have the privilege of experiencing the things we do because we have the gift of now, we just don’t appreciate it because we are too busy listening to others and what should be happening and all that, which is fair we are a product of our environments. But you’ll notice after you’ve poured into all those other aspects you always come back, because there’s a reason you were there and felt so strongly to begin with, we just don’t take the time to understand why we do the things we do and where certain things genuinely apply like when it’s time to make life changing decisions, like career changes, moving countries, breaking up with people in your life’s, we do the things that are the easiest for us at the moment that’s just how we are as humans, but sometimes we play it too safe you know, it’s about striking a good balance, a little bit of risk and a little bit of playing it safe, I think you know when you see something being an avenue for growth but you might not feel ready, when if not now? Fight for what you believe in but understand why you’re fighting why you’re there so you can have the drive to keep pushing just like heidi said in this video.

I’ve been noticing lately how much self-judgments that push me to go against my natural energy are making things harder for me. I left a community not long ago that I outgrew, and I’m currently in a season of venturing out to some new communities and finding new mentors to continue growing in a couple areas that are important to me. Now that I’m meeting a lot of new people, I’m bumping into my own temperament of being slow to warm up to people and needing a lot of energy to process new people and places, until I become comfortable opening up. I noticed all these self-judgments came online about why can’t I grow up and be comfortable just talking to new people, or why can’t I push through a bunch of new experiences instead of having to rest a bunch in between. I realized that these judgments were making it even harder and more exhausting being around people. I’m trying now to lean into accepting the pace that works for me and seeing if I can accept that I might need and want to observe and hang back for a while before I open up to people. Looking to see if I could do that with a more calm energy rather than an energy of self-judgment.

From my mine of work and other in it I've talked to. If you have over bearing supervisors that treat you poorly you burn out faster. It is often called Retired On duty. But if your boss treats you fairly and shows they care and has your back, engages in ideas it happens less often. A study was done on factory workers. The lighting was poor. So the factory bosses tried to fix the lights to increase productivity. After three or four lighting improvements work increased each time. They asked why the workers had improved. And the consensus was "W e complained about the lighting and the company did something." Shocked, because they thought it was the natural lighting and a softer light that was better the fluorescence. But showing you care about employees is the most helpfull.

It’s crazy how burnout doesn’t just feel like “being tired”, it feels like your brain and body are permanently stuck in survival mode. For me, it was endless scrolling at night, zero focus during the day, and this weird numbness where even rest didn’t feel like rest. I honestly thought something was broken in me. The turning point was stumbling on Reset Your Body and Mind by Morgan Lane. It wasn’t some rigid “discipline” thing it was a gentle 30-day reset that actually worked in real life. I didn’t realize how much energy and clarity I was missing until I felt it again. You really don’t know what you’re leaving on the table until you try. If you’re in that burnout loop, just start with Day 1. Give yourself the reset. You’ll feel the shift sooner than you expect.

Thank you. I have stumbled across your channel whilst ironically procrastinating and I have never found material such as yours, which addresses daily struggles and provides supportive recommendations in such a way not to make the viewer feel stupid, lazy or useless. I have made an appointment to see my doctor to be screened for ADHD. I have always knows there is something different about the way I function but I could never put my finger on it. On one of your videos, you mentioned ADHD has been occasionally misdiagnosed as depression or other mental health conditions. I have been taking anti-depressants for 5 years as per the doctors advice. I felt run down (burnout), unfulfilled and directionless as my goals or aspirations changed on a weekly basis. This, coupled with the inability to concentrate on monotonous or arduous tasks led to said diagnosis of depression. I've lost 3 jobs in the past due to productivity and inability to manage my tasks so this all hits home so hard. I am excited to have started "the ball rolling" on my journey to a more accurate diagnosis even though it took me 4 days to pick up the phone and make the initial call. Thank you for being so personable and fantastic with your explanations and to-the-point videos for helping me understand where I may be on terms of brain function. You genuinely may have just changed my life and for that I will be forever grateful! x

My burnout experience was pretty violent. I spiraled downward slowly for several months, dealing with unstable home relationships, a job closing down, I left to take care of myself and landed my own place because the chaos from others was too much. But as my job left me, I was hitting a bad spot. I took on two jobs at once, but fell behind on rent. All the unresolved pressure eventually broke me down. I noticed something was wrong when I began making more illogical choixes. I felt moments of confusion and panic. The big day was when I just stopped. I quit all my jobs and isolated myself. Had a 5 day fever, my body was so sensitive like I was literally fried out. I didn't eat for 32 hours. I was a shivering wreck. But talking with a friend after isolating for 5 days, I slowly picked up all the pieces. However, that experience affected me for a long time. I felt the effects of this for a whole year, even long after things improved and I got my life steady and functioning. 70% of what caused this problem was not having money... It always goes back to not enough money.

It was practically impossible to take care of ourselves. The depersonalization was real. Even dissociation at some points. I'm trying very hard to dig out of it now, while also grieving very intensely. I do have a few missions however that's helping keep going. One is to hike his flag to the top of MT. Washington. The other, is at 42, go back to school to become a therapist. His passing, as horrible as it is, is making that a possibility. It's been in my plans for years as I've been on my journey being a survivor of clergy abuse. ( Only realized it at 37) It was part of his wishes for me to go forth and help people and for once in my life, I feel I have purpose. Anyhow, great video. Thank you!

Em đồng ý với quan điểm của anh Trí. Em từng có một khoảng thời gian lo sợ và overthinking về rất nhiều thứ xung quanh mình. Tin tốt là thời diểm hiện tại em đã tìm được trạng thái bình yên. Dù em vẫn overthinking gần như là mỗi ngày. Nhưng giống như anh Trí đã trình bày, em có bộ lọc suy nghĩ để điều hướng dòng chảy trong đầu mình: "Thứ gì mình tác động được, thay đổi được thì lo mà hành động đi. Còn thứ gì mình không ảnh hưởng được thì hoặc tìm cách tận dụng nó, hoặc mặc nó, lo chi tổ mệt." 😁 Một cách khác mà em áp dụng song song là: Tự tranh luận với chính mình. Em cho phép mọi dòng duy nghĩ trái chiều nhau hay thậm chí không liên quan cùng đồng thời chạy. Đúng là đa số trường hợp chúng vả nhau tới tấp khiến em hết sức mệt mỏi. Nhưng kết quả sau những cuộc tranh luận như thế hoặc là thuyết phục thành công vấn đề không đáng suy ngẫm nữa -> Bình an; hoặc là thành công giải quyết vấn đề theo cách tối ưu nhất; hoặc thậm chí là tìm ra những phương pháp mới, những góc nhìn mới nữa. Nói chung là không thiệt thòi gì cả. Vấn đề lớn nhất em gặp phải có chăng là việc để nhiều luồng suy nghĩ tự do va chạm nhau như thế, em sẽ rất khó diễn giải ra trọn vẹn ý kiến của mình cho người khác. Nhiều lúc khá phiền nhưng em thấy chấp nhận được khi tâm mình bình an.😇 Rất cảm ơn anh Trí và đội ngũ ekip đã cho ra video ý nghĩa này ạ.💕💕

My lessons: 1. Exam pressure is often exaggerated — one failure does not ruin your life. 2. Everyone must face their own challenges; others cannot live life for you. 3. Make sure the “mountain” you’re climbing is your choice, not someone else’s expectation. 4. Real life and earning money are much harder than studying. 5. Time is the most valuable asset — don’t waste it on unnecessary pressure. 6. Economic and financial knowledge is essential for life and family security. 7. Understanding money helps you avoid manipulation and social conflict. 8. Learn about finance: money management, inflation, good vs. bad debt, banking, and business. 9. Life quality depends on the quality of your daily choices.

Finding a hobby is great, but it’s more important to identify the root cause of emotional burnout. If you’re burnt out because you work in a toxic or abusive environment, finding a hobby isn’t going to cure anything. It might bring temporary relief (in the same way as a glass of wine), but you’ll continue to burn out. Genuine lifestyle changes - especially in the job category - may be the actual cure.

Lessons from 190 The fear of loss often comes from the ego holding you back. Ask yourself why you fear it, why you act that way, and why you keep repeating the same patterns — to understand yourself better. Give yourself time to feel the pain, honor it, and allow yourself to be vulnerable, but only for a while. You will heal in time, because suffering is a normal part of life.

Small note regarding the "this is the anxiety speaking" when talking about having too many tasks to do at a time. Unfortunately, for fields like IT this is the normal expectation and there are smaller daily tasks or emergencies that need to be handled regularly or even at unexpected times. This is a field where financially we are already quite privileged, so from my perspective it's pretentious or just a huge privilege to assume most people could make decisions about their weekly workload, time schedules or deadlines/ focus times for specific tasks. This might actually be the reason for poor mental health in this field, but I doubt most office workers have actual agency about these things, or kind and emotionally understanding bosses or work in companies with enough staff so that their workload is actually manageable. They are keeping us "comfortably" in the zone just before burnout, so they need to pay less wages and squeeze every bit of productivity out of us. Resisting this and setting boundaries might get you fired even when you are more productive this way. It doesn't matter to the higher ups of a conpany.

After working 100+ hours a week for the better part of a decade (and getting screamed at by a client when I took a day off for my birthday), a divorce, and four traumatic events in a row, I had a complete nervous breakdown. I haven't been the same since. I'm ill a lot and have panic attacks around the idea of going back to full-time work. I miss overworking because it made me feel like I was a worthwhile person. Now that I can't, I struggle to find purpose.

As a desk worker, highly consider outdoor or physical hobbies because it brings the balance to your life to be doing something with your body / hands if your work has a high mental load. Gardening, hiking, joining a running/walking group or work out group, pottery, dance class, playing the drums or yoga. The more full body experience the better to fuel the mind-body connection and get you out of your head.

This is my version of Carson x Dio story.. We were friends since grade 8..had a great memories with him..hindi ko na iisa-isahin. Basta masasabi ko he is the kind of bestfriend that will not make you feel bad about yourself. Though may mga criticisms siya na minsan di ko matanggap kasi pikunin ako eh and afterwards naman mare-realize ko that he is right, i know that he just want me to grow as a person. i love how he always corrects me pag i do make mistakes. i love how he mocks me when i do make bad decisions especially sa pag-amin sa mga taong gusto ko.We had a great platonic relationship. not until, i dreamed of him siguro lagi ko nakikita pagmu-mukha niya and he was just really there when i needed him the most. So, after HS graduation. Summer na. April 1. I admit to him thru chat that I have a crush on him. Kaso the feelings is not mutual. pero i accepted na agad. parang yung confession ko non we didn't take it seriously. we decided to be still be friends di naman ako non matitiis. since summer, di ko naman na siya nakikita kaya i assume na nawala na rin agad yung feelings ko...not until again classmates kami nung SHS. I always witness him liking other girls and wondering why i do have some weird feelings na bakit ako nasasaktan yung ganon i keep denying what i really felt with myself. As a respect dun sa MU niya na which is our classmate i distance myself to him and I've known being his gbf kasi eh, even though hindi naman raw kailangan. I divert my attention to someone else. Pero paminsan minsan he is keeping in touch with me. In fact pinababasa niya sakin yung mga tula na ginawa niya for her MU para tanungin kung okay lang ba. It is more than enough for me to see him happy with her. Kaso yun nga lang they didn't last long together. he just loved that person so much. he got so wasted mula don. syempre ako si tanga. i comforted a bestfriend. after months..we got so close w/ each other naging clingy and we end up entering fwb thing ang daming nangyayari eh heartbroken siya, i have problem with my fam and parang yun yung naging solusyon sa mga problema namin. he doesn't know that i had feelings for him while we are doing it, even though we made a deal na walang feelings dapat na ma-involve. eventually after a month, i end up confessing to him that i do love him na. First reaction, he got mad why i didn't tell him in the first place before we did the fwb. Lastly, he doesn't feel the same way although he tried to love me sabi niya and i deserve better raw. After that incident, things get blurry na di ko na alam nangyari. Pero ang alam ko lang there's a lot crying that happened it lasted for a month. Sa CR, sa Kwarto. Matagal tagal na self-pity ang nangyari. Hanggang sa napunta ako ngayon dito sinusulat to. Crazy how much plans I used to make w/ that person I no longer talk to. And Now, I realized Sa sobrang kilala namin isa't-isa we knew what kind of love we deserve for the both of us..pero nagkataon lang siguro na we are not the right person for each other. Pero di pa rin ako maka-graduate sa kaniya. He is my first love. He was my first at everything. But, It is what it is ika nga. One thing is for sure i can never unloved you. Oh Kay, tagal pa kitang mamahalin even if is not with me.

The bit about being too focused on one area of our lives to the detriment of the others and thus our bodies hitting a natural limit? Whew! My mom is recently retired and she has theeee hardest time relaxing and/or doing anything recreational b/c she was a workaholic for 40 years! And yep, that was 40 years of work while her body also hit several natural limits that she still deals with to this day (joint issues, high BP, etc.). I love and admire her and her tenacity so much and at the same time feel for her b/c her generation (and those prior) just ground themselves down to the stump with work and I just wish she could allow herself to have that actual, relaxing retirement experience while she still can, you know? You worked all these years, lady; enjoy it! 😅

Compassion fatigue is a real thing and even worse when you’re waaaayyy underpaid and overworked. Like there’s only so much time in a day to complete tasks and when you’re literally outnumbered by patient to staff ratio then it’s basically a nonstop shit show. You go into work and bust your ass only to spend any days off recovering (literally) from all the exhaustion only to return to work the next day and do it all over again. This is no way to live. And the real kicker is when these helping fields advertise education as “rewarding”. Like really? It’s depressing to see someone go into the field motivated, then after about 10 years it’s like wow…this is what I CHOSE to give my life to? Smfh just awful.

One thing I like about my job is that I can work almost whenever I want (tho I need to be reachable during some regular working hours). I too sometimes like to work on weekends. I don't do much work, but like starting a day with 1-3 hours of work does not really use much of my time and I may even get decent results in those hours, as I don't work out of necessity, but because I am in the correct mood. But this leads to another issue: We have way too much work. Most of us don't like our work and most of the hours is the kind of work that leaves us exhausted. And even when we like it, 8 hours is way too much. It is just an useless time waster. We should drastically reduce working hours. And no, we don't need to reduce pay. Just look at graphs that show how we are greatly more efficient than ever, but we are paid pretty much the same type of money than in the 80's, when the shift to efficiency happened. Workers deserve more. Investors and CEOs should stop leaching our results. Especially investors creeate absolutely nothing.

I have had the opportunity of taking almost 6 sabbatical years in my life (and I'm not even 30), not because I wanted, but because I was basically forced to take them due to my "outstanding" health... I know that not many people are able to take them and most are forced to keep studying and working and praying that they don't drop dead from exhaustion any day, but still somehow I feel very "behind" in life due to barely being able to finish university and with almost zero work experience, yet most people my age (mid to late 20s) are starting to reach "milestones" such as having a stable job, a business, a proper career, buying houses, cars, being able to afford nicer things. I know that I cannot overwork myself unless I want to die somewhat young or maybe middle aged, so I know I have to seek a career that allows me to have a modest lifestyle while not wearing myself down. Because I know if I did a demanding job, eventually I will have to spend all the money earned in hospital bills.

I'm so burned out that I'm abandoning friendships in a desperate attempt to have more time for myself. Honestly, if I never interacted in-person with another human being in my life, it would be too fucking soon.

I know that medical workers are a good example of burnout but us teachers/childcare providers are also suffering immensely from burnout. Teaching is an incredibly underpaid, overworked, and unappreciated job and it’s terrifying the lack of empathy that slowly creeps in after years of dealing with parents that don’t care about their children, admin that only care about profits/cutting costs, and coworkers that are also losing their empathy… it’s sad and scary and I every day I feel myself losing my passion for my job bc I can’t let go of my ideals of what we should be doing to support children based of current studies, which just simply will never change in my lifetime bc the ppl in charge care about the status quo from the old days.

Been a developer for 20 years. Hit major burnout three times. It's absolutely a real thing and will build up unbreakable walls. I will stare at my computer for hours at a time without a single keystroke. Its like a mental battlefield and you will lose most battles. Each burnout period took roughly three months for me to break out of and generally cost one job in the process. I'm currently in my third burnout. And I think this might be my breaking point. I'm nearing the point of dropping my 20 year career and it breaks my heart. Burnout will not only affect what is causing the burnout, like a job, but every other aspect of your life. If anyone else is burnt out and suffering, know you are not alone.

Damn, this is the best description of burn out and I felt attacked directly. I love what I actually do, and get paid reasonably well, my life isn’t completely awful, I really like my peers, and I feel bad for complaining… but I’m declining financially slightly, doing less of the things I love at work, the workload keeps me from the things I love outside of work, I lack control where I need it, and the person I’m doing it for is literally making 10x what I am from my labor while not caring about the things I care most about… yeah, I’m burned out AF.

Ugh burnout. I experienced burnout during the pandemic as people were laid off and no one to replace them. In turn, I did double the work and almost doubled my hours to complete said work. On top of it, I was going to school part time. I attempted to address it with my boss and was told, everyone is going through it and sometimes we have to give up what we want to get by. So, I had to drop my classes to carry this workload. As time went on my workload increased. I did eventually have a mental breakdown. My point is because I never successfully addressed it, I developed gastritis and what my doctor said was gastroparesis this past January. It's now the end of September and I just recovered from the gastritis and a little bit of the gastroparesis has resolved. I'm thinking I'll be 100 percent by the end of the year. Address burnout because not only can burnout manifest itself mentally it can manifest physically. The first few months of coming down with this were scary, I had no clue what was happening to my body and lost 15 pounds.

I kept wondering if I was depressed. I wasn’t sad just tired, unmotivated, numb. Turns out, it wasn’t depression. It was burnout from constant overstimulation. Reset Your Body and Mind by Morgan Lane helped me see the difference and more importantly, heal from it. That 30-day reset brought my energy, sleep, and focus back things I didn’t even realize I lost. If you’re not sure what’s wrong, start with a reset. You’ll feel the shift.

I actually quit my job 9 months ago because of burnout. I actually liked what I did and I was good at it. But that feeling being exhausted all the time, never having energy for myself and family, I couldn't deal with it any more. I have no regrets leaving that job. I would absolutely do that work again, I would even work at the same place, but the time away has given me time to realise I just didn't have the capacity to do everything that was asked of me at work and still have something left in "the tank" for home and myself. That word capacity is a big one I got to understand this year. There are people who have the capacity to do what I did and still have energy after for their personal lives, me definitely not. So now I'm looking for work in the same area, but part time instead of full time. I'm looking forward to finding a good work/life balance.

I worked for 15 years in the systems area of ​​insurance companies and from the first day I felt that I had to justify my salary, that I had to be perfect and never burn out and always be grateful for the reward of working. But one day I got burned out and couldn't take it anymore and I just told myself "relax, I'll get you out of here" and when a manager asked me about my situation I just told them that I wanted to run out of the building. Today I live happily without bosses, without orders, without deadlines. I have goats and quails and they give me peace and happiness. I no longer care about money or pretending anything to anyone, I just try to be happy.

A few points though: 1. you can do worst tasks early in the week or as soon as it appears. It's called "eating the frog". If you are employed on 40h contract you shouldn't need to work on your weekends. Just do it during your work time and don't let it occupy your mind by postponing it. 2. Using of subconscious is a good hack, but it is a two way street: if you DON'T want to remember something just cram it into one day and use the method for things you do want to affect you on more fundamental level.

I've been in burnout many times, and I think this current one started about 4-5 months ago. But life is extremely stressful at the moment, so there is no rest for a while yet, I have no choice but to push through, again. Next week I will ask my doctor to send me to get a diagnosis for CPTSD, because it seems like many of my bodily issues can be explained by that, and as one fellow said, "If you grew up with undiagnosed autism and decades of trauma at home, in school and in the workplace, how can you NOT have CPTSD?" When I got out of a 16 year depression a few years back, I thought I'd solved life, I knew how to handle it, boy was I wrong. It was a nice vacation, and now I will get back to work on myself, armed with more knowledge and confidence, and hopefully some professional help🤞
